title: Europe's AI Infrastructure Giant: Nebius Is Building the GPU Empire You Haven't Noticed
subtitle: With $700M from Accel and Nvidia, a Paris mega-cluster, and revenue scaling past $1B+, Nebius is quietly becoming Europe's answer to CoreWeave.
summary: Nebius Group (NBIS) raised $700M from Accel and Nvidia in March 2026, launched a 20,000-H100 Paris cloud region, and locked in a multi-year deal with a major US hyperscaler estimated at $150M+ ARR. Revenue hit $399M in Q1 2026 alone — on pace for $1.5B+ annualized — and the stock has surged from its $12 IPO to ~$215, reflecting a ~$54.5B market cap. The European AI infrastructure play is getting very real, very fast.

image.caption: Nebius data center infrastructure in Finland. The Amsterdam-based company is rapidly expanding its GPU cloud footprint across Europe to meet surging AI workload demand.

bodyHtml:
There's a quiet GPU empire being built in Amsterdam — and it's expanding faster than most people realize. Nebius Group (NASDAQ: NBIS) is not the flashiest name in AI infrastructure, but the numbers say it should be.

Revenue trajectory: ~$80M in 2024 → ~$530M in 2025 → Q1 2026 alone hit $399M, putting the company on pace for $1.5B+ annualized. That's nearly 19x growth from 2024 to 2026 run rate. And the catalysts keep stacking up.

The $700M Inflection Point

In March 2026, Nebius closed a $700M private placement backed by Accel and Nvidia. Let that sink in — Nvidia itself is cutting checks to Nebius. That's a signal, not just capital.

The raise wasn't about survival. Nebius had $3.7B+ in cash on its balance sheet as of year-end 2025. This was about acceleration. The company is building out GPU capacity at a pace that rivals CoreWeave and Lambda, with $6.5B+ in net PP&E already deployed, but with a distinctly European flavor.

Paris: 20,000 H100s and Counting

February 2026 marked the launch of the Nebius Paris cloud region — a 20,000 H100 GPU cluster that instantly made it one of the largest AI compute providers in Europe. The timing is impeccable: European enterprises are scrambling for sovereign AI infrastructure that doesn't require shipping data to US-based clouds.

The European AI sovereignty narrative is real. Regulations like the EU AI Act are pushing enterprises toward local compute. Nebius, headquartered in Amsterdam with data centers across Finland and now France, is the natural beneficiary.

Mistral AI + Hugging Face = Ecosystem Lock-In

Nebius isn't just building hardware. It's building an ecosystem. The partnership with Mistral AI — Europe's leading open-source AI lab — supplies 5,000+ H100 GPUs for training and inference. That's Mistral's home-field advantage meeting Nebius's infrastructure play.

Then there's the Hugging Face integration. Nebius is positioned as Hugging Face's preferred GPU provider, giving it direct access to millions of ML engineers. Every developer deploying on Hugging Face gets a seamless path to Nebius compute. That's distribution that money can't buy overnight.

The Hyperscaler Deal That Changes Everything

Here's the one that doesn't get enough attention: Nebius signed a multi-year deal with a major US hyperscaler, estimated at $150M+ in annual recurring revenue. The hyperscaler isn't named publicly, but the implications are clear — the biggest players in cloud are treating Nebius as a strategic capacity partner, not a competitor.

Think about what that means: a company that did ~$530M in total revenue in FY2025 just locked in a contract worth roughly a quarter of that on a single-deal annual basis. These are the kinds of deals that compound.

The Stock: Up 1,690% From IPO

NBIS went public at ~$12 in October 2024, and now trades around $215 with a ~$54.5B market cap. The stock has delivered an extraordinary 1,690% return from its IPO price, with a 52-week range of $34.72 to $233.73. At this price, NBIS trades at roughly 36x forward sales — not cheap, but reflecting the massive demand for AI compute capacity.

CEO Arkady Volozh (founder of Yandex, Europe's largest tech company before sanctions reshuffled the deck) is a battle-tested operator who has built at scale before. The team knows how to run a capital-intensive infrastructure business, and the return on equity has turned positive at 14.1%.

The Risks

It's not all H100s and hockey sticks. Nebius faces real headwinds: the GPU supply chain remains constrained, competition from US hyperscalers is intensifying, and the European regulatory environment is unpredictable. The stock is also thinly traded compared to US-listed peers, which means volatility is baked in — and at an 80%+ gross margin but still-negative operating margins, profitability is still ahead.

And there's the geopolitical angle. Volozh's Yandex history means Nebius is under extra scrutiny. The company has gone to great lengths to distance itself from Russia, recapitulating in Amsterdam with clean corporate governance, but the overhang is real.

The Bottom Line

Nebius is the AI infrastructure bet for investors who want European exposure, CEO-led execution, and a valuation that reflects the scale of the opportunity. The $700M Accel/Nvidia raise, the Paris mega-cluster, the hyperscaler deal — these aren't hypotheticals. They're signed contracts and deployed hardware.

If AI workloads continue migrating from experimentation to production (and every signal says they will), companies like Nebius that own the physical compute layer will be essential. The GPU landlord thesis works — and Nebius is building the best portfolio in Europe.

Disclaimer: This content is for informational purposes only and does not constitute financial advice. Always do your own research.
